title: SEGMENT 6 — Kidney organoids: Melissa Little, Morizane, and the problem of nephrons

lines.text: SEGMENT 6 — Kidney organoids: Melissa Little, Morizane, and the problem of nephrons
The kidney is, mechanically, one of the more impressive feats of organoid engineering.
The reason is that kidney architecture is intricate.

A real human kidney contains about a million nephrons — the functional units of the kidney — and each nephron is a tube with multiple distinct segments: glomerulus, proximal tubule, loop of Henle, distal tubule, collecting duct.
Each segment does different chemistry.
Each segment has different cell types.
And the whole thing is woven together with a vast blood supply, because the kidney's job is to filter blood.
Building this in a dish, even in miniature, is a serious challenge.
Two groups, working in parallel, cracked the kidney organoid problem in the mid-2010s.
The first was Melissa Little's group, originally in Brisbane, Australia, now in Melbourne.
Little had been working on kidney development for years.

In 2015, her group published a paper in Nature showing that you could take human pluripotent stem cells through a carefully timed sequence of differentiation cues — first inducing mesoderm with Wnt activation, then specifying intermediate mesoderm (the embryonic precursor of the kidney) with FGF, then transitioning the cells into kidney developmental programs — and end up with organoids containing recognizable nephrons with proper segmentation.
Glomerulus-like structures with podocytes.
Proximal tubules with brush borders.
Distal tubules.
Collecting duct-like structures.

The organoids weren't whole kidneys — they were collections of dozens to hundreds of nephrons in a clump, without a real ureter or pelvis to drain to — but they were nephron-like enough to be a genuine model of kidney tissue.
The second group, working roughly in parallel, was Ryuji Morizane's, then in Joseph Bonventre's lab at Harvard.
Morizane developed a slightly different protocol with somewhat different timing but achieved a similar result: pluripotent stem cell-derived organoids with structured nephrons.
The two protocols differ in their details but converge on similar end products, and labs around the world now use one or both depending on their experimental needs.
What can you do with a kidney organoid?
Quite a bit, actually.
You can model genetic kidney diseases.
Polycystic kidney disease — PKD — is a common inherited disorder where the kidneys develop fluid-filled cysts that gradually destroy kidney function.
If you derive iPSCs from a PKD patient and make kidney organoids, the organoids develop cysts.
You can watch the disease form, in a dish, in real time.
You can test drugs that might block cyst formation.
You can do gene editing on the iPSCs to correct the mutation and see if the corrected organoids stop forming cysts (they do).
This is the kind of mechanistic experiment you can do in an organoid that you simply cannot do in a real kidney.
You can do nephrotoxicity screening.
The kidney is one of the most common targets of drug toxicity, because it filters everything in your blood and concentrates it.
Many drug candidates fail late in development because they turn out to be kidney-toxic.
Kidney organoids let you screen for nephrotoxicity earlier, in human tissue, before committing to expensive animal studies and clinical trials.
You can study acute kidney injury — the kidney damage caused by ischemia, sepsis, or toxic insults.
Organoids can be subjected to controlled injuries and you can watch the molecular and cellular responses, identify which pathways drive damage versus recovery, and look for therapeutic targets.

And there's longer-term interest in using kidney organoids for transplantation — ultimately growing replacement kidney tissue from a patient's own cells, to address the chronic shortage of donor kidneys.
That dream is still far away, but pieces are being assembled.
Vascularized kidney organoids transplanted into mice can integrate with host vasculature and develop more mature nephrons.
Some groups are working on assembling larger kidney structures from multiple organoids fused together.
The path is long but visible.
I want to make a small philosophical point about the kidney work, because it generalizes.
Kidney organoids are imperfect models.
They lack the proper ureteric and collecting duct architecture in many cases.
They lack mature vasculature.
The cells in them are often immature compared to adult kidney cells, more resembling fetal kidney.
You cannot, today, take a kidney organoid and use it as a stand-in for an adult human kidney in most contexts.
But you can use it as a model of developing human kidney.
You can use it to ask questions that no other system lets you ask, because real human fetal kidneys are not available for experimentation.
You can use it as a starting point that captures real human cell biology where mouse models don't.
You can use it as a substrate for asking specific questions: how does this genetic mutation affect nephron formation?
Which drug damages this particular tubule segment?
What kinds of cells are present and what are they doing?
This is the right way to think about organoids in general.
They are not replacements for real organs.
They are partial recapitulations — models that capture some aspects of real biology, miss others, and are useful precisely insofar as you understand what they capture and what they don't.
A good organoid researcher knows the limitations of their system as well as its strengths.
We'll come back to this when we discuss what organoids can't do.
For now, on to the next organ.
